speaker: interviewer
text: Could you write letters to your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the beginning, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you receive letters and news?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, in the beginning. Yes, in the beginning.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who wrote you? Who wrote you back? You were writing to your family.

speaker: interviewee
text: To my family, right.

speaker: interviewer
text: To your mother and father? Who wrote you back?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did they say? Do you remember what they were saying in the letters?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they couldn't say too much, because--

speaker: interviewer
text: It was postcards.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were censored. Censored.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was censored.

speaker: interviewee
text: Censored. I still have it. I can show you.

speaker: interviewer
text: But-- but you did receive some letters, some postcards at the beginning?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, in the beginning. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember how long you stayed in that camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Four years.

speaker: interviewer
text: You stayed four years in that same camp, from-- from 1942--

speaker: interviewee
text: Not in the same, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: To '45, you stayed in that same camp, doing the same kind of work? Or did you change work?

speaker: interviewee
text: We changed work, because in the diffrerent camp. In the first camp, I was in a-- in a-- a kitchen?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. You were peeling potatoes, and then you worked in a kitchen. The second camp, you worked in a factory.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you shaved? Did they-- did they let you keep your hair?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. They let me keep my hair.

speaker: interviewer
text: They let you keep your hair. What kind of uniforms did you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had no uniform.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had your own clothes?

speaker: interviewee
text: My own clothes, yes,

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any Jewish stars to show that you were--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did they put the Jewish stars?

speaker: interviewee
text: One in the back and one in the front.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the back of your-- in front? Not on your-- on your skirt or your dress?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you had to go to work, or you walked to work?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How-- do you remember how long the walk was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe an hour.

speaker: interviewer
text: An hour? What about role calls? Were you on role calls in the morning, being counted?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes, we were being counted. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you remember how long that roll call was?

speaker: interviewee
text: A half an hour.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, when they take you to-- when they take you to-- to the factory, the SS women guard, they had dogs with them, or were they just-- how were they-- how were they guarding you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only the SS women. There were no dogs by us, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: But not-- not with dogs?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: They didn't have any dogs.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Not in this camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said to me that some of them were not good, and some of them were good. How were they good?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they overlooked things. They overlooked something. They talked to us friendly. And some of them were going with the thing-- with the--

speaker: interviewer
text: The whip?

speaker: interviewee
text: Whip. And didn't say a word to us, and chased us, 'faster faster.' That's how I mean. And some of them overlooked things. And some of them just yelled, to make them feel their power.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you keep your morale? How did you keep lying? What made you keep alive?

speaker: interviewee
text: When we went to work, there were French soldiers there in another camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were prisoners of war?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were prisoners. Right. And they had the rooms. They lived upstairs, like an upper floor. And when we went by from our camp to work, they looked down the window, and they let us know on their hands how the war was coming along. And sometimes they're throwing down little leaflets to us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the SS guards, they didn't do anything? They let you read them?

speaker: interviewee
text: They couldn't see everything, you know. So we grabbed that, and we were reading it in the barrack.

speaker: interviewer
text: How big a camp was it? Do you remember how big a camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not a big camp, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was not a big camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: And there were only women there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only women, right. In the first camp where I was, they were mixed.

speaker: interviewer
text: But the last one in Czechoslovakia was not-- was not-- it was just women.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was just women, yes. The one day, I was with my husband there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where-- where were you with your husband? What camp were you where you were--

speaker: interviewee
text: Gross Sarne.

speaker: interviewer
text: Gross Sarne?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you-- how did you see him? Remember when you first saw him?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was-- at that time, I wasn't-- didn't know him so much.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were working in the kitchen, right?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was working the kitchen, and I gave out some meals. So when he took another meal, I didn't look. I didn't see it. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I got two-- two--

speaker: interviewee
text: He took two plates.

speaker: interviewee
text: Two plates.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's how you remember him from the first camp? Yes. Yes. And then we went-- sometimes we went out, and they had-- we had a bigger room where we socialized.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the first camp, in Gross Sarne?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, in Gross Sarne. We used to come there, and we used to know together the people, so--

speaker: interviewer
text: You had something in common with him, because you were both Polish?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then-- then you left, and you didn't know what happened to him, right?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I think I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you went to the camp in Czechoslovakia, you don't know where he went?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. And then we met again, when the war was over.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you stayed in that second camp until you were liberated, until 1945?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Until May?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what the--

speaker: interviewee
text: May 8.

speaker: interviewer
text: May 8, 1945, you were liberated?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: By whom?

speaker: interviewee
text: By the Russians.

speaker: interviewer
text: By the Russian soldiers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hm.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was that day?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh--

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me.

speaker: interviewee
text: That woman who was watching us, she jumped on the table high. She said, 'oh, you're free. You're free!' We didn't do nothing to her. She wasn't so bad. She just was yelling.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was that SS guard woman?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes

speaker: interviewer
text: Who said, 'you're free, you're free?'

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. But some other ones, they were not so good. But the ones who watched us were not so bad.

speaker: interviewer
text: But what-- what happened when she said, 'you're free. You're free?' What did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing to her. We didn't do nothing to her. We just were--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you-- did you go out of the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were-- we were just dancing and crying. [LAUGHS] And then we started to look for family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you look?

speaker: interviewee
text: And somebody came. I know my husband from the camp, from the-- with the men, where we were. And a girlfriend of mine came, because she was there visiting that camp with the men, because she had a brother there. So she said-- she came back to the women's camp, and she said, 'do you know who was there? I've seen this and this,' I thought names which I knew. And I know my husband's name.

speaker: interviewer
text: You recognized the name of your future husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was not your husband then.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. And he came-- I don't remember if he came with somebody else. He came to take us out from there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, he was liberated the same day you were liberated, wasn't he?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Were you? May the 8th?

speaker: interviewer
text: May 8, 1945. He was liberated the same-- the same day.

speaker: interviewee
text: But he was in a different camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. And when did you-- when you saw him again-- when did you see him again?

speaker: interviewee
text: A week later [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: When the war was over.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. You saw him a week later, after the liberation?

speaker: interviewee
text: That could be. I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what-- what did you do after the liberation? Where did you go? You didn't stay in the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: He took us-- he took us-- her brother, my girlfriend's brother-- and Henry took us away to a different city.

speaker: interviewer
text: I didn't understand who was there before Henry, your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. My girlfriend's brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your girlfriend's brother.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And took us out from the camp. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And where we were in that house, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was looking out through the window, and there was a friend passing by. And she said, you know you have a brother? And I said, where? I didn't know anything where the brother was, who lived, who died. I didn't know nothing. So she said-- I said to her, where? And she told me that he was-- he's still in Poland, Bytom. So naturally, I said, I have to go. And so Henry came along.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know which brother it was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. My oldest. He's alive, thank God.

speaker: interviewer
text: Chaim?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hirsch. Hirsch.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hershel.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hershel.

speaker: interviewee
text: The oldest.

speaker: interviewee
text: My oldest.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was the oldest brother. What about the rest--

speaker: interviewee
text: He was my-- he was the second oldest.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, the oldest-- the oldest brother. Of all the brothers, he was the oldest person. The oldest one of all your brothers.

speaker: interviewee
text: He, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know what happened to the rest of your family, to your mother and father and to your other sister and brothers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I presumed they took them to-- to Auschwitz. I didn't know. I wasn't home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your brother tell you anything about it? Did he know anything about them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, they took them. He knew. He had lost-- my brother lost a wife and a child, too, in there. But he told me that-- who went away.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were all deported? Now, where--

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother went away with a younger brother of mine they took.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they were sent to Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Probably. I never seen her again.

speaker: interviewer
text: You never saw any other-- except for your brother, you didn't see anybody else in your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, where did your brother go? Did he go in camp? He was arrested and put to camps also?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was in Birkenau.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. He was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Herschell was in Birkenau.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your brother was sent to Birkenau but survived. And you went with Henry to see him after the war, in Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was your first reunion?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened then?

speaker: interviewee
text: What happened then? We came to America. Where did he go?

speaker: interviewee
text: He went to Israel.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, to Israel, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: He went to Israel.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he lives in Israel? He's still alive?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Yes, he's alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: But he lives where?

speaker: interviewee
text: He lives in Canada.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Canada. Now where-- where did you-- after you saw your brother, where did you go after that?

speaker: interviewee
text: We all got together.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: We lived together.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you go after that? After you saw him? How long did you stay there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Beuthen.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, in Beuthen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Beuthen is where, in Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bytom. Bytom. Now belongs to Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: But it was in Germany at that time.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was German, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Henry was always with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Who?

speaker: interviewer
text: Your husband, Henry, was always with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you get married, then, in Beuthen?

speaker: interviewee
text: We married-- no, in Bamberg. In Bamberg.

speaker: interviewer
text: You got married in-- you were in Bamberg and got married there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you have-- don't you have a child born in-- in Bamberg?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not in Bamberg. Who was born in Bamberg?

speaker: interviewee
text: Samuel.

speaker: interviewee
text: Samuel was born in Bamberg?

speaker: interviewer
text: Your son, Samuel, was born in Bamberg.

speaker: interviewee
text: The oldest, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were waiting to come to the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. Well, Henry went to the Army, the American Army. And that's how we could come here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you happy to come to the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course. Of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you learn to speak English before?

speaker: interviewee
text: Before in Germany? No. Just picked up words. So from when some soldiers came to our house and things, and picked up from movies, picked up a few words, but really not.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you arrive in the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: When we arrived? '49.

speaker: interviewee
text: July 1949.

speaker: interviewer
text: In 1949. Did you like living in the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course. Did you get used to it right away? Or how long did it take you to get used to it? It took me fast.

speaker: interviewer
text: Very fast?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go first?

speaker: interviewee
text: New Haven.

speaker: interviewer
text: New Haven?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. New Haven, Connecticut.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you make friends there? You didn't have any families living in the United States, did you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, who sent you here? Who sent you? Was it an organization?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the name of the organization?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Joint.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Joint?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they helped you to settle yourself in the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now did you work when you arrived in the United States? Or were you still a housewife?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I had a child.

speaker: interviewer
text: You took care of your child?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go from-- from New Haven?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: You went to-- to Detroit? Didn't you go to Detroit after New Haven?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know which came first. To Detroit we went?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, you went to Detroit.

speaker: interviewee
text: I guess.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you enjoy living in Detroit?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Wasn't bad.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you stay in Detroit?

speaker: interviewee
text: For a long time.

speaker: interviewer
text: A long time? Do you have-- do you have another child? Do you have a-- how many children do you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: Three.

speaker: interviewer
text: You have three. You have two daughters.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they were both born in Detroit?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember Barbara was born in Detroit. I don't remember Pam. Where was Pam born? Detroit?

speaker: interviewer
text: Pam was born in Detroit also.

speaker: interviewee
text: So--

speaker: interviewer
text: You had a good life?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, didn't complain.

speaker: interviewer
text: And are they-- are they all married now, your children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just Pam is married.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your son?

speaker: interviewee
text: And my son is married.

speaker: interviewer
text: But your younger daughter is not married?

speaker: interviewee
text: My older daughter is not married.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your older daughter is not married?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. She's kind of a little bit fussy. [LAUGHS] And that was--

speaker: interviewer
text: You moved to Laguna Hills in California when your husband--

speaker: interviewee
text: Retired.

speaker: interviewer
text: Retired.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you enjoying your life in Laguna Hills?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. I like it very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you see your children or grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. They're coming here, I'm going there. We have some friends here, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you-- do you think sometimes or often about the Holocaust, what you went through?

speaker: interviewee
text: I try not to.

speaker: interviewer
text: But when you think of--

speaker: interviewee
text: When we speak, when we get together.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me what you-- what you say when you get together and then talk-- speak about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when our friends start telling me this and that, she had this, remember this one, remember that one. So we start. That's how it starts.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you used to get nightmares from it sometimes?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of nightmares did you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: That somebody's chasing us, and the whips, and things like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mean like soldiers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Soldiers and the guards there, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what do you try to do in those nightmares?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sometimes I cried out, and sometimes nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just wake up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Turned around. My brother was very bad.

speaker: interviewer
text: In which way?

speaker: interviewee
text: He used to scream. Terrible.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you see your brother now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, when we were living there, before California.

speaker: interviewer
text: Does he have a family also?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yeah. He lost a wife in the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Before the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: Before the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: He lost a wife before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: What do you mean before the war? I don't know.

speaker: interviewee
text: He lost-- he lost a wife, you know, the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh! But he had the second wife.

speaker: interviewee
text: Before he went to camp, yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: So what happened to her?

speaker: interviewer
text: He lost his second wife also?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure. The camp. Where is-- OK, where is the second wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: Second wife, she's living. Paula.

speaker: interviewee
text: Paula is the second wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sorry. Couldn't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's OK. Is there anything else you want to add that you didn't tell me? Let me ask you. Do you think-- do you think something like this, that you went through, can happen again?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: Depends all on the people. Same thing. By Hitler-- before Hitler in Germany, it was very good for the Jewish people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before Hitler.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Germany was good for Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's all right. So then now Hitler came. So that's turned on. The same here. That's up to the people. If they let somebody like Hitler come in, it can happen. But I don't think so, because America is America. But you never know. One never knows.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think America is America because there are too many people from different nationalities?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is that why?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. In there-- over there, they didn't know. Just they know Jewish people, German people. They didn't know [INAUDIBLE]. Too many. So that's what happened. They start out with the Jewish people. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Yeah, so it wasn't so good.

speaker: interviewer
text: You know what we're going to do? We're going to show some photos of your-- before and during the war, and after the war. And I want to thank you for leaving your testimony. Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you for taking it.

speaker: interviewee
text: So this is the picture of Chava Yoskowitz. This picture was taken in 1939. And she tells me that was her father's favorite picture.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was taken where?

speaker: interviewee
text: That thing was taken in Benjen, Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that's your wife? That's your-- Henry--

speaker: interviewee
text: My future wife.

speaker: interviewer
text: Henry Wellner, that's your wife.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's-- that's my wife, Henry Wellner.

speaker: interviewee
text: Eva Wellner.

speaker: interviewee
text: Eva Wellner. This is a picture of my wife's sister. She was born in 19--

speaker: interviewee
text: '14. And she was 20 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was in 1934? What is her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name is Chana-- Chana Yoskowitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was taken where?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was taken in Benjen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Poland. This picture was taken-- we went to visit Salla Gornschak. She's in the middle. It was taken in 1947 in Ansbach, Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where is your wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: Those three women were together with me in concentration camp Gross Sarne. And after the liberation, I brought them-- took them all out from Schatzlar and brought them to Waldenburg. And we were friends up to now. My wife--

speaker: interviewer
text: Now tell me who's on the right.

speaker: interviewee
text: My wife, Chava Wellner, is on the right.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the middle?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the middle is Salla Gornschak. She lives in New York. And Frieda Silverstein. She passed away two years ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Eva, they were all with you in the camps?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were all with me in the camp in--

speaker: interviewer
text: In Gross Sarne?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Gross Sarne. And they-- we lived together in Waldenburg, and we met again in Schatzlar, Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, did you tell me what year that was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1947. This is one of the postcards we were allowed to write up to 1943.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who-- who wrote that postcard?

speaker: interviewee
text: This postcard was sent to--

speaker: interviewee
text: From home

speaker: interviewee
text: To my wife while she in Schatzlar in-- in Czechoslovakia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is Chaim?

speaker: interviewee
text: The name Chaim Israel Yoskowitz--

speaker: interviewee
text: My father.

speaker: interviewee
text: --was her father. See, Israel wasn't actually his name. Every Jew had to adopt a name Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's like Eva. Eva Sara was an adopted name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And Eva Sara. And a woman had to-- a woman had to adopt the name 'Sara,' to make sure that this from a Jewish person.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now what does the Z mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Z means censored. That means the guard looked it over or read it over. If you-- every time you sent out a postcard or we received a postcard, it had to go through the censors. And the Z stands for censors.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Eva, you received many postcards like this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, yes, but not for long.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, what kind of document is that?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is not a document.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me what it is.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is-- they wrote a card on my birthday, my friends in the concentration camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Schlatzlar?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Schlatzlar. And they gave it to me, and I hold onto it for so many years. It was very important.

speaker: interviewee
text: She--

speaker: interviewee
text: On my birthday.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's written in Polish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Wish 100-- she wish 100 years of life. And she wrote not only for herself, she wrote for all of the girls. And she wished they-- that she could have good luck and be hope-- be liberated soon. And-- and then she says, we're wishing you the same thing, good health and-- and prosperity and should have a lot of--

speaker: interviewee
text: Freedom.

speaker: interviewee
text: Should have a lot of luck. And she says, and it's just four years [INAUDIBLE]. [POLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Which means what?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yours-- yours.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sincerely?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sincerely.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sincerely.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sincerely, love?

speaker: interviewee
text: Your friends.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sincerely love, Sally.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now when you say 'she,' who-- who was it that wrote this letter?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sally Gornschak.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sally, OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, Salla [? Grunenbaum. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Sally who?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Grunenbaum. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Sally [? Grunenbaum. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: Grunenbaum. [NON-ENGLISH]. Gornsckak is-- This picture was taken in 1942 in Benjen, Poland. This is one of the brothers--

speaker: interviewer
text: Of Eva?

speaker: interviewee
text: From Eva Yoskowitz. And she had four-- she had five brothers. Only one survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what's his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name--

speaker: interviewee
text: Hershel.

speaker: interviewee
text: -is Isaak Yoskowitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Isaak Yoskowitz.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, Hershel.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hershel was the first one. That's Isaal. Hershel is--

speaker: interviewee
text: Which one who lived? What's his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE]. we're talking about this one. This is a certificate translated from-- from German to English.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's a birth certificate?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's a birth certificate of my son, Chaim Simon Wellner. So it says Simon Wellner, born April 20, 1947, in Bamberg, Germany. And on the bottom it says Henry Wellner is automobile mechanic. Last residency was in Beuthen, Helmovskiego 7. And the next one, his mother, Eva Wellner, born Joskowitz, Jewish, last residence in Sossnowitz, and now both at this time in Bamberg, Gabelsbergerstrasse [GERMAN]-- 18. And this certifies the translation from German to English for my son's birth certificate. This picture was taken in our house in Southfield, Michigan, in 1984. That's me, Henry Wellner, on the right, my wife, Eva Wellner, on the left, and my oldest daughter, Barbara Ann Wellner, sitting on the chair. This picture was taken in our house in Southfield, Michigan. And this is the brother of my wife, Hershel Yoskowitz. He's the only brother who survived in concentration camp. He was in Birkenau, And he was working for an [? IG farm. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is next to him?

speaker: interviewee
text: And next to him is his wife, Paula-- Paula Yoskowitz. Her maiden name was Paula [? Kamiski. ?] She was born in Sosnowiec, Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that's Eva Wellner's brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Eva Wellner's-- yeah, Eva Wellner's brother, only brother who survived. This picture was taken in Southfield, Michigan in 1985. Here are my two daughters. On the right is Pamela Wellner. And on the middle, there's my wife.

speaker: interviewer
text: Eva?

speaker: interviewee
text: Eva Wellner. And on the left is Barbara Ann Wellner. This picture was taken in our house in 19-- in Southfield, Michigan in 1984. On the right is Michael Wellner. On the right-- the upper-- is Jody Wellner, and on the left is Lisa Wellner.

speaker: interviewer
text: And those are your grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: And those are my three grandchildren.

speaker: interviewer
text: From your-- from your son's.

speaker: interviewee
text: From my-- from my son, Simon Wellner.
